revision.text:
{{Infobox ancient site | name = Ryūgakuji kofun cluster | native_name =龍角寺古墳群 | native_name_lang = | image = Iwaya-kofun-zenkei.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Iwaya kofun in the Ryūgakuji kofun cluster | map_type = Japan Chiba Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ryūgakuji kofun cluster | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|35|49|16|N|140|16|38|E|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Sakae, Chiba|Sakae]] / [[Narita, Chiba|Narita]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kantō region]] | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (park and museum) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
{{nihongo|'''Ryūgakuji kofun cluster'''|龍角寺古墳群|Ryūgakuji kofun-gun}} is a group of [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|burial mound]]s located in what is now the Ryukakuji neighborhood of the town of [[Sakae, Chiba|Sakae]] and extending into the city of [[Narita, Chiba|Narita]], [[Chiba Prefecture]] in the [[Kantō region]] of [[Japan]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1941, with the area extended in 2009.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/211990|title=龍角寺古墳群・岩屋古墳 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref> Containing 114 tumuli, it is the largest cluster of ''kofun'' in the Kantō region. The National Historic Site designation also singles out Kofun No. 105, which is named the {{nihongo|'''Iwaya kofun'''|岩屋古墳|}}.
==Overview== The Ryūgakuji kofun cluster dates from the late Kofun period (end of the 6th century to the end of the 7th century AD), and is located on what was once the northeastern coast of Lake Inbanuma. The area is part of the Shimōsa Plateau with an elevation of about 30 meters above sea level. The site is relatively narrow, and the tumuli are distributed in strips over the narrow plateau from the northwest to the southeast for about 1.5 kilometers. The total number of tumuli currently confirmed is 114, although many may have been destroyed by road construction and urban encroachment, and others may yet to be discovered.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> The types of these 114 tumuli are distributed as follows:
{| class="wikitable" |+ Ryūgakuji Kofun Cluster |- ! Type !! Narita !! Sakae !! Total |- | {{Ill|Round Kofun|lt=Domed|ja|円墳}} ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳}}) || 19 || 52 || 71 |- | {{ill|Square Kofun|lt=Square|ja|方墳}} ({{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳}}) || 1 || 5 || 6 |- | [[Zenpokoenfun|Keyhole]] ({{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳}}) || 9 || 28 || 37 |- | TOTAL || 29 || 85 || 114 |- |}
Most of the tumuli are well preserved and more than 70 percent are located on prefectural lands. At present, the oldest is believed to be scallop-shaped Kofun No.101, which was constructed in the second quarter of the 6th century AD based on the type of ''[[haniwa]]'' discovered. The later mounds are concentrated in the northeast corner of the site, and include the Sengenyama Kofun, which is believed to be the last ''kofun'' built in Chiba Prefecture. From these dates, it is clear that the tumulus-building culture continued well after the introduction of [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]] to the area in the [[Hakuho period]] as evidenced by the nearby ruins of the [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple]] of [[Ryūkaku-ji]]. Many of the tumuli are small, with a length of 20 to 30 meters in the case of keyhole-shaped tumuli, and a diameter of 10 to 20 meters in the case of many dome-shaped burial mounds.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
Some of the larger mounds were found to have more than one burial chamber, or had been reused for a subsequent burial some period after its original construction. ''Haniwa'' have been found only at 16 tumuli, but the majority of the tumuli remain unexcavated.
Artifacts recovered from the site are exhibited at the {{nihongo|'''Chiba Prefectural Bōsō Mura no Fudoki no Oka Museum'''|千葉県立房総のむら風土記の丘資料館|Chiba-kenritsu Bōsō Mura no Fudoki no Oka Shiryōkan}}. The site is a five minute walk from the Fudoki no Oka Kitaguchi bus stop on the Chiba Kotsu Bus from [[Ajiki Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Narita Line]]<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Iwaya Kofun== The Iwaya Kofun is Kofun No. 105 in the cluster. It is a square-sided tumulus {{nihongo|''{{ill|Square Kofun|lt=hōfun|ja|方墳}}''|方墳}}) and is the largest tumulus in the cluster. The tumulus is approximately 78 meters on each side, and is built in three tiers similar to a step pyramid, with a total height of 13 meters. Inside are two lateral-type stone-lined [[burial chamber]]s constructed of blocks of soft limestone containing many fossilized shells. The tumulus has a double moat, enclosing an area measuring 108 meters east-to-west by 96 meters north-to-south. Since the entrance has been open since the [[Edo period]], no grave goods have been discovered.
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Chiba)]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{commons Category:kk]][[Category:-inline|Ryukakuji Kofun Group}} *[https://www.pref.chiba.lg.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunkazai/n411-017.html Chiba Prefecture official site] {{in lang|ja}} *[http://www.town.sakae.chiba.jp/index.php?code=874 Sakae Town official home page] {{in lang|ja}} *[http://www2.chiba-muse.or.jp/www/MURA/contents/1519312671176/index.html Chiba Prefectural Bōsō Mura no Fudoki no Oka Museum] {{in lang|ja}}

Some of the larger mounds were found to have more than one burial chamber, or had been reused for a subsequent burial some period after its original construction. ''Haniwa'' have been found only at 16 tumuli, but the majority of the tumuli remain unexcavated.
Artifacts recovered from the site are exhibited at the {{nihongo|'''Chiba Prefectural Bōsō Mura no Fudoki no Oka Museum'''|千葉県立房総のむら風土記の丘資料館|Chiba-kenritsu Bōsō Mura no Fudoki no Oka Shiryōkan}}. The site is a five minute walk from the Fudoki no Oka Kitaguchi bus stop on the Chiba Kotsu Bus from [[Ajiki Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Narita Line]]<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Iwaya Kofun== The Iwaya Kofun is Kofun No. 105 in the cluster. It is a square-sided tumulus {{nihongo|''{{ill|Square Kofun|lt=hōfun|ja|方墳}}''|方墳}}) and is the largest tumulus in the cluster. The tumulus is approximately 78 meters on each side, and is built in three tiers similar to a step pyramid, with a total height of 13 meters. Inside are two lateral-type stone-lined [[burial chamber]]s constructed of blocks of soft limestone containing many fossilized shells. The tumulus has a double moat, enclosing an area measuring 108 meters east-to-west by 96 meters north-to-south. Since the entrance has been open since the [[Edo period]], no grave goods have been discovered.

{{Infobox ancient site | name = Ryūgakuji kofun cluster | native_name =龍角寺古墳群 | native_name_lang = | image = Iwaya-kofun-zenkei.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Iwaya kofun in the Ryūgakuji kofun cluster | map_type = Japan Chiba Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ryūgakuji kofun cluster | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|35|49|16|N|140|16|38|E|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Sakae, Chiba|Sakae]] / [[Narita, Chiba|Narita]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kantō region]] | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (park and museum) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
{{nihongo|'''Ryūgakuji kofun cluster'''|龍角寺古墳群|Ryūgakuji kofun-gun}} is a group of [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|burial mound]]s located in what is now the Ryukakuji neighborhood of the town of [[Sakae, Chiba|Sakae]] and extending into the city of [[Narita, Chiba|Narita]], [[Chiba Prefecture]] in the [[Kantō region]] of [[Japan]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1941, with the area extended in 2009.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/211990|title=龍角寺古墳群・岩屋古墳 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref> Containing 114 tumuli, it is the largest cluster of ''kofun'' in the Kantō region. The National Historic Site designation also singles out Kofun No. 105, which is named the {{nihongo|'''Iwaya kofun'''|岩屋古墳|}}.
==Overview== The Ryūgakuji kofun cluster dates from the late Kofun period (end of the 6th century to the end of the 7th century AD), and is located on what was once the northeastern coast of Lake Inbanuma. The area is part of the Shimōsa Plateau with an elevation of about 30 meters above sea level. The site is relatively narrow, and the tumuli are distributed in strips over the narrow plateau from the northwest to the southeast for about 1.5 kilometers. The total number of tumuli currently confirmed is 114, although many may have been destroyed by road construction and urban encroachment, and others may yet to be discovered.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> The types of these 114 tumuli are distributed as follows:
{| class="wikitable" |+ Ryūgakuji Kofun Cluster |- ! Type !! Narita !! Sakae !! Total |- | {{Ill|Round Kofun|lt=Domed|ja|円墳}} ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳}}) || 19 || 52 || 71 |- | {{ill|Square Kofun|lt=Square|ja|方墳}} ({{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳}}) || 1 || 5 || 6 |- | [[Zenpokoenfun|Keyhole]] ({{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳}}) || 9 || 28 || 37 |- | TOTAL || 29 || 85 || 114 |- |}
Most of the tumuli are well preserved and more than 70 percent are located on prefectural lands. At present, the oldest is believed to be scallop-shaped Kofun No.101, which was constructed in the second quarter of the 6th century AD based on the type of ''[[haniwa]]'' discovered. The later mounds are concentrated in the northeast corner of the site, and include the Sengenyama Kofun, which is believed to be the last ''kofun'' built in Chiba Prefecture. From these dates, it is clear that the tumulus-building culture continued well after the introduction of [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]] to the area in the [[Hakuho period]] as evidenced by the nearby ruins of the [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple]] of [[Ryūkaku-ji]]. Many of the tumuli are small, with a length of 20 to 30 meters in the case of keyhole-shaped tumuli, and a diameter of 10 to 20 meters in the case of many dome-shaped burial mounds.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
Some of the larger mounds were found to have more than one burial chamber, or had been reused for a subsequent burial some period after its original construction. ''Haniwa'' have been found only at 16 tumuli, but the majority of the tumuli remain unexcavated.
Artifacts recovered from the site are exhibited at the {{nihongo|'''Chiba Prefectural Bōsō Mura no Fudoki no Oka Museum'''|千葉県立房総のむら風土記の丘資料館|Chiba-kenritsu Bōsō Mura no Fudoki no Oka Shiryōkan}}. The site is a five minute walk from the Fudoki no Oka Kitaguchi bus stop on the Chiba Kotsu Bus from [[Ajiki Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Narita Line]]<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Iwaya Kofun== The Iwaya Kofun is Kofun No. 105 in the cluster. It is a square-sided tumulus {{nihongo|''{{ill|Square Kofun|lt=hōfun|ja|方墳}}''|方墳}}) and is the largest tumulus in the cluster. The tumulus is approximately 78 meters on each side, and is built in three tiers similar to a step pyramid, with a total height of 13 meters. Inside are two lateral-type stone-lined [[burial chamber]]s constructed of blocks of soft limestone containing many fossilized shells. The tumulus has a double moat, enclosing an area measuring 108 meters east-to-west by 96 meters north-to-south. Since the entrance has been open since the [[Edo period]], no grave goods have been discovered.

{{Infobox ancient site | name = Ryūgakuji kofun cluster | native_name =龍角寺古墳群 | native_name_lang = | image = Iwaya-kofun-zenkei.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Iwaya kofun in the Ryūgakuji kofun cluster | map_type = Japan Chiba Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ryūgakuji kofun cluster | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|35|49|16|N|140|16|38|E|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Sakae, Chiba|Sakae]] / [[Narita, Chiba|Narita]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kantō region]] | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (park and museum) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
{{nihongo|'''Ryūgakuji kofun cluster'''|龍角寺古墳群|Ryūgakuji kofun-gun}} is a group of [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|burial mound]]s located in what is now the Ryukakuji neighborhood of the town of [[Sakae, Chiba|Sakae]] and extending into the city of [[Narita, Chiba|Narita]], [[Chiba Prefecture]] in the [[Kantō region]] of [[Japan]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1941, with the area extended in 2009.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/211990|title=龍角寺古墳群・岩屋古墳 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref> Containing 114 tumuli, it is the largest cluster of ''kofun'' in the Kantō region. The National Historic Site designation also singles out Kofun No. 105, which is named the {{nihongo|'''Iwaya kofun'''|岩屋古墳|}}.
==Overview== The Ryūgakuji kofun cluster dates from the late Kofun period (end of the 6th century to the end of the 7th century AD), and is located on what was once the northeastern coast of Lake Inbanuma. The area is part of the Shimōsa Plateau with an elevation of about 30 meters above sea level. The site is relatively narrow, and the tumuli are distributed in strips over the narrow plateau from the northwest to the southeast for about 1.5 kilometers. The total number of tumuli currently confirmed is 114, although many may have been destroyed by road construction and urban encroachment, and others may yet to be discovered.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> The types of these 114 tumuli are distributed as follows:
{| class="wikitable" |+ Ryūgakuji Kofun Cluster |- ! Type !! Narita !! Sakae !! Total |- | {{Ill|Round Kofun|lt=Domed|ja|円墳}} ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳}}) || 19 || 52 || 71 |- | {{ill|Square Kofun|lt=Square|ja|方墳}} ({{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳}}) || 1 || 5 || 6 |- | [[Zenpokoenfun|Keyhole]] ({{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳}}) || 9 || 28 || 37 |- | TOTAL || 29 || 85 || 114 |- |}
Most of the tumuli are well preserved and more than 70 percent are located on prefectural lands. At present, the oldest is believed to be scallop-shaped Kofun No.101, which was constructed in the second quarter of the 6th century AD based on the type of ''[[haniwa]]'' discovered. The later mounds are concentrated in the northeast corner of the site, and include the Sengenyama Kofun, which is believed to be the last ''kofun'' built in Chiba Prefecture. From these dates, it is clear that the tumulus-building culture continued well after the introduction of [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]] to the area in the [[Hakuho period]] as evidenced by the nearby ruins of the [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple]] of [[Ryūkaku-ji]]. Many of the tumuli are small, with a length of 20 to 30 meters in the case of keyhole-shaped tumuli, and a diameter of 10 to 20 meters in the case of many dome-shaped burial mounds.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
Some of the larger mounds were found to have more than one burial chamber, or had been reused for a subsequent burial some period after its original construction. ''Haniwa'' have been found only at 16 tumuli, but the majority of the tumuli remain unexcavated.
Artifacts recovered from the site are exhibited at the {{nihongo|'''Chiba Prefectural Bōsō Mura no Fudoki no Oka Museum'''|千葉県立房総のむら風土記の丘資料館|Chiba-kenritsu Bōsō Mura no Fudoki no Oka Shiryōkan}}. The site is a five minute walk from the Fudoki no Oka Kitaguchi bus stop on the Chiba Kotsu Bus from [[Ajiki Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Narita Line]]<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Iwaya Kofun== The Iwaya Kofun is Kofun No. 105 in the cluster. It is a square-sided tumulus {{nihongo|''{{ill|Square Kofun|lt=hōfun|ja|方墳}}''|方墳}}) and is the largest tumulus in the cluster. The tumulus is approximately 78 meters on each side, and is built in three tiers similar to a step pyramid, with a total height of 13 meters. Inside are two lateral-type stone-lined [[burial chamber]]s constructed of blocks of soft limestone containing many fossilized shells. The tumulus has a double moat, enclosing an area measuring 108 meters east-to-west by 96 meters north-to-south. Since the entrance has been open since the [[Edo period]], no grave goods have been discovered.
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Chiba)]]

==Overview== The Ryūgakuji kofun cluster dates from the late Kofun period (end of the 6th century to the end of the 7th century AD), and is located on what was once the northeastern coast of Lake Inbanuma. The area is part of the Shimōsa Plateau with an elevation of about 30 meters above sea level. The site is relatively narrow, and the tumuli are distributed in strips over the narrow plateau from the northwest to the southeast for about 1.5 kilometers. The total number of tumuli currently confirmed is 114, although many may have been destroyed by road construction and urban encroachment, and others may yet to be discovered.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> The types of these 114 tumuli are distributed as follows:
{| class="wikitable" |+ Ryūgakuji Kofun Cluster |- ! Type !! Narita !! Sakae !! Total |- | {{Ill|Round Kofun|lt=Domed|ja|円墳}} ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳}}) || 19 || 52 || 71 |- | {{ill|Square Kofun|lt=Square|ja|方墳}} ({{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳}}) || 1 || 5 || 6 |- | [[Zenpokoenfun|Keyhole]] ({{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳}}) || 9 || 28 || 37 |- | TOTAL || 29 || 85 || 114 |- |}
Most of the tumuli are well preserved and more than 70 percent are located on prefectural lands. At present, the oldest is believed to be scallop-shaped Kofun No.101, which was constructed in the second quarter of the 6th century AD based on the type of ''[[haniwa]]'' discovered. The later mounds are concentrated in the northeast corner of the site, and include the Sengenyama Kofun, which is believed to be the last ''kofun'' built in Chiba Prefecture. From these dates, it is clear that the tumulus-building culture continued well after the introduction of [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]] to the area in the [[Hakuho period]] as evidenced by the nearby ruins of the [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple]] of [[Ryūkaku-ji]]. Many of the tumuli are small, with a length of 20 to 30 meters in the case of keyhole-shaped tumuli, and a diameter of 10 to 20 meters in the case of many dome-shaped burial mounds.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
Some of the larger mounds were found to have more than one burial chamber, or had been reused for a subsequent burial some period after its original construction. ''Haniwa'' have been found only at 16 tumuli, but the majority of the tumuli remain unexcavated.
Artifacts recovered from the site are exhibited at the {{nihongo|'''Chiba Prefectural Bōsō Mura no Fudoki no Oka Museum'''|千葉県立房総のむら風土記の丘資料館|Chiba-kenritsu Bōsō Mura no Fudoki no Oka Shiryōkan}}. The site is a five minute walk from the Fudoki no Oka Kitaguchi bus stop on the Chiba Kotsu Bus from [[Ajiki Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Narita Line]]<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Iwaya Kofun== The Iwaya Kofun is Kofun No. 105 in the cluster. It is a square-sided tumulus {{nihongo|''{{ill|Square Kofun|lt=hōfun|ja|方墳}}''|方墳}}) and is the largest tumulus in the cluster. The tumulus is approximately 78 meters on each side, and is built in three tiers similar to a step pyramid, with a total height of 13 meters. Inside are two lateral-type stone-lined [[burial chamber]]s constructed of blocks of soft limestone containing many fossilized shells. The tumulus has a double moat, enclosing an area measuring 108 meters east-to-west by 96 meters north-to-south. Since the entrance has been open since the [[Edo period]], no grave goods have been discovered.
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Chiba)]]

{{nihongo|'''Ryūgakuji kofun cluster'''|龍角寺古墳群|Ryūgakuji kofun-gun}} is a group of [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|burial mound]]s located in what is now the Ryukakuji neighborhood of the town of [[Sakae, Chiba|Sakae]] and extending into the city of [[Narita, Chiba|Narita]], [[Chiba Prefecture]] in the [[Kantō region]] of [[Japan]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1941, with the area extended in 2009.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/211990|title=龍角寺古墳群・岩屋古墳 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref> Containing 114 tumuli, it is the largest cluster of ''kofun'' in the Kantō region. The National Historic Site designation also singles out Kofun No. 105, which is named the {{nihongo|'''Iwaya kofun'''|岩屋古墳|}}.
==Overview== The Ryūgakuji kofun cluster dates from the late Kofun period (end of the 6th century to the end of the 7th century AD), and is located on what was once the northeastern coast of Lake Inbanuma. The area is part of the Shimōsa Plateau with an elevation of about 30 meters above sea level. The site is relatively narrow, and the tumuli are distributed in strips over the narrow plateau from the northwest to the southeast for about 1.5 kilometers. The total number of tumuli currently confirmed is 114, although many may have been destroyed by road construction and urban encroachment, and others may yet to be discovered.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> The types of these 114 tumuli are distributed as follows:

Many of the tumuli are small, with a length of 20 to 30 meters in the case of keyhole-shaped tumuli, and a diameter of 10 to 20 meters in the case of many dome-shaped burial mounds.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
Some of the larger mounds were found to have more than one burial chamber, or had been reused for a subsequent burial some period after its original construction. ''Haniwa'' have been found only at 16 tumuli, but the majority of the tumuli remain unexcavated.
Artifacts recovered from the site are exhibited at the {{nihongo|'''Chiba Prefectural Bōsō Mura no Fudoki no Oka Museum'''|千葉県立房総のむら風土記の丘資料館|Chiba-kenritsu Bōsō Mura no Fudoki no Oka Shiryōkan}}. The site is a five minute walk from the Fudoki no Oka Kitaguchi bus stop on the Chiba Kotsu Bus from [[Ajiki Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Narita Line]]<ref name="Shiseki"/>

Some of the larger mounds were found to have more than one burial chamber, or had been reused for a subsequent burial some period after its original construction. ''Haniwa'' have been found only at 16 tumuli, but the majority of the tumuli remain unexcavated.
Artifacts recovered from the site are exhibited at the {{nihongo|'''Chiba Prefectural Bōsō Mura no Fudoki no Oka Museum'''|千葉県立房総のむら風土記の丘資料館|Chiba-kenritsu Bōsō Mura no Fudoki no Oka Shiryōkan}}. The site is a five minute walk from the Fudoki no Oka Kitaguchi bus stop on the Chiba Kotsu Bus from [[Ajiki Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Narita Line]]<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Iwaya Kofun== The Iwaya Kofun is Kofun No. 105 in the cluster. It is a square-sided tumulus {{nihongo|''{{ill|Square Kofun|lt=hōfun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}''|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}) and is the largest tumulus in the cluster. The tumulus is approximately 78 meters on each side, and is built in three tiers similar to a step pyramid, with a total height of 13 meters. Inside are two lateral-type stone-lined [[burial chamber]]s constructed of blocks of soft limestone containing many fossilized shells. The tumulus has a double moat, enclosing an area measuring 108 meters east-to-west by 96 meters north-to-south. Since the entrance has been open since the [[Edo period]], no grave goods have been discovered.

revision.comment: Adding [[Category:前方後円墳]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:前方後円墳]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Infobox ancient site | name = Ryūgakuji kofun cluster | native_name =龍角寺古墳群 | native_name_lang = | image = Iwaya-kofun-zenkei.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Iwaya kofun in the Ryūgakuji kofun cluster | map_type = Japan Chiba Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ryūgakuji kofun cluster | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|35|49|16|N|140|16|38|E|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Sakae, Chiba|Sakae]] / [[Narita, Chiba|Narita]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kantō region]] | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (park and museum) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
{{nihongo|'''Ryūgakuji kofun cluster'''|龍角寺古墳群|Ryūgakuji kofun-gun}} is a group of [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|burial mound]]s located in what is now the Ryukakuji neighborhood of the town of [[Sakae, Chiba|Sakae]] and extending into the city of [[Narita, Chiba|Narita]], [[Chiba Prefecture]] in the [[Kantō region]] of [[Japan]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1941, with the area extended in 2009.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/211990|title=龍角寺古墳群・岩屋古墳 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref> Containing 114 tumuli, it is the largest cluster of ''kofun'' in the Kantō region. The National Historic Site designation also singles out Kofun No. 105, which is named the {{nihongo|'''Iwaya kofun'''|岩屋古墳|}}.
==Overview== The Ryūgakuji kofun cluster dates from the late Kofun period (end of the 6th century to the end of the 7th century AD), and is located on what was once the northeastern coast of Lake Inbanuma. The area is part of the Shimōsa Plateau with an elevation of about 30 meters above sea level. The site is relatively narrow, and the tumuli are distributed in strips over the narrow plateau from the northwest to the southeast for about 1.5 kilometers. The total number of tumuli currently confirmed is 114, although many may have been destroyed by road construction and urban encroachment, and others may yet to be discovered.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> The types of these 114 tumuli are distributed as follows:
{| class="wikitable" |+ Ryūgakuji Kofun Cluster |- ! Type !! Narita !! Sakae !! Total |- | {{Ill|Round Kofun|lt=Domed|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun}}) || 19 || 52 || 71 |- | {{ill|Square Kofun|lt=Square|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} ({{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}) || 1 || 5 || 6 |- | [[Zenpokoenfun|Keyhole]] ({{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳}}) || 9 || 28 || 37 |- | TOTAL || 29 || 85 || 114 |- |}
Most of the tumuli are well preserved and more than 70 percent are located on prefectural lands. At present, the oldest is believed to be scallop-shaped Kofun No.101, which was constructed in the second quarter of the 6th century AD based on the type of ''[[haniwa]]'' discovered. The later mounds are concentrated in the northeast corner of the site, and include the Sengenyama Kofun, which is believed to be the last ''kofun'' built in Chiba Prefecture. From these dates, it is clear that the tumulus-building culture continued well after the introduction of [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]] to the area in the [[Hakuho period]] as evidenced by the nearby ruins of the [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple]] of [[Ryūkaku-ji]]. Many of the tumuli are small, with a length of 20 to 30 meters in the case of keyhole-shaped tumuli, and a diameter of 10 to 20 meters in the case of many dome-shaped burial mounds.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
Some of the larger mounds were found to have more than one burial chamber, or had been reused for a subsequent burial some period after its original construction. ''Haniwa'' have been found only at 16 tumuli, but the majority of the tumuli remain unexcavated.
Artifacts recovered from the site are exhibited at the {{nihongo|'''Chiba Prefectural Bōsō Mura no Fudoki no Oka Museum'''|千葉県立房総のむら風土記の丘資料館|Chiba-kenritsu Bōsō Mura no Fudoki no Oka Shiryōkan}}. The site is a five minute walk from the Fudoki no Oka Kitaguchi bus stop on the Chiba Kotsu Bus from [[Ajiki Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Narita Line]]<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Iwaya Kofun== The Iwaya Kofun is Kofun No. 105 in the cluster. It is a square-sided tumulus {{nihongo|''{{ill|Square Kofun|lt=hōfun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}''|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}) and is the largest tumulus in the cluster. The tumulus is approximately 78 meters on each side, and is built in three tiers similar to a step pyramid, with a total height of 13 meters. Inside are two lateral-type stone-lined [[burial chamber]]s constructed of blocks of soft limestone containing many fossilized shells. The tumulus has a double moat, enclosing an area measuring 108 meters east-to-west by 96 meters north-to-south. Since the entrance has been open since the [[Edo period]], no grave goods have been discovered.
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Chiba)]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{commons Category:kk]][[Category:-inline|Ryukakuji Kofun Group}} *[https://www.pref.chiba.lg.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunkazai/n411-017.html Chiba Prefecture official site] {{in lang|ja}} *[http://www.town.sakae.chiba.jp/index.php?code=874 Sakae Town official home page] {{in lang|ja}} *[http://www2.chiba-muse.or.jp/www/MURA/contents/1519312671176/index.html Chiba Prefectural Bōsō Mura no Fudoki no Oka Museum] {{in lang|ja}}

Artifacts recovered from the site are exhibited at the {{nihongo|'''Chiba Prefectural Bōsō Mura no Fudoki no Oka Museum'''|千葉県立房総のむら風土記の丘資料館|Chiba-kenritsu Bōsō Mura no Fudoki no Oka Shiryōkan}}. The site is a five minute walk from the Fudoki no Oka Kitaguchi bus stop on the Chiba Kotsu Bus from [[Ajiki Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Narita Line]]<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Iwaya Kofun== The Iwaya Kofun is Kofun No. 105 in the cluster. It is a square-sided tumulus {{nihongo|''{{ill|Square Kofun|lt=hōfun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}''|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}) and is the largest tumulus in the cluster. The tumulus is approximately 78 meters on each side, and is built in three tiers similar to a step pyramid, with a total height of 13 meters. Inside are two lateral-type stone-lined [[burial chamber]]s constructed of blocks of soft limestone containing many fossilized shells. The tumulus has a double moat, enclosing an area measuring 108 meters east-to-west by 96 meters north-to-south. Since the entrance has been open since the [[Edo period]], no grave goods have been discovered.

{{Infobox ancient site | name = Ryūgakuji kofun cluster | native_name =龍角寺古墳群 | native_name_lang = | image = Iwaya-kofun-zenkei.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Iwaya kofun in the Ryūgakuji kofun cluster | map_type = Japan Chiba Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ryūgakuji kofun cluster | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|35|49|16|N|140|16|38|E|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Sakae, Chiba|Sakae]] / [[Narita, Chiba|Narita]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kantō region]] | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (park and museum) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
{{nihongo|'''Ryūgakuji kofun cluster'''|龍角寺古墳群|Ryūgakuji kofun-gun}} is a group of [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|burial mound]]s located in what is now the Ryukakuji neighborhood of the town of [[Sakae, Chiba|Sakae]] and extending into the city of [[Narita, Chiba|Narita]], [[Chiba Prefecture]] in the [[Kantō region]] of [[Japan]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1941, with the area extended in 2009.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/211990|title=龍角寺古墳群・岩屋古墳 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref> Containing 114 tumuli, it is the largest cluster of ''kofun'' in the Kantō region. The National Historic Site designation also singles out Kofun No. 105, which is named the {{nihongo|'''Iwaya kofun'''|岩屋古墳|}}.
==Overview== The Ryūgakuji kofun cluster dates from the late Kofun period (end of the 6th century to the end of the 7th century AD), and is located on what was once the northeastern coast of Lake Inbanuma. The area is part of the Shimōsa Plateau with an elevation of about 30 meters above sea level. The site is relatively narrow, and the tumuli are distributed in strips over the narrow plateau from the northwest to the southeast for about 1.5 kilometers. The total number of tumuli currently confirmed is 114, although many may have been destroyed by road construction and urban encroachment, and others may yet to be discovered.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> The types of these 114 tumuli are distributed as follows:
{| class="wikitable" |+ Ryūgakuji Kofun Cluster |- ! Type !! Narita !! Sakae !! Total |- | {{Ill|Round Kofun|lt=Domed|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun}}) || 19 || 52 || 71 |- | {{ill|Square Kofun|lt=Square|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} ({{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}) || 1 || 5 || 6 |- | [[Zenpokoenfun|Keyhole]] ({{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}) || 9 || 28 || 37 |- | TOTAL || 29 || 85 || 114 |- |}
Most of the tumuli are well preserved and more than 70 percent are located on prefectural lands. At present, the oldest is believed to be scallop-shaped Kofun No.101, which was constructed in the second quarter of the 6th century AD based on the type of ''[[haniwa]]'' discovered. The later mounds are concentrated in the northeast corner of the site, and include the Sengenyama Kofun, which is believed to be the last ''kofun'' built in Chiba Prefecture. From these dates, it is clear that the tumulus-building culture continued well after the introduction of [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]] to the area in the [[Hakuho period]] as evidenced by the nearby ruins of the [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple]] of [[Ryūkaku-ji]]. Many of the tumuli are small, with a length of 20 to 30 meters in the case of keyhole-shaped tumuli, and a diameter of 10 to 20 meters in the case of many dome-shaped burial mounds.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
Some of the larger mounds were found to have more than one burial chamber, or had been reused for a subsequent burial some period after its original construction. ''Haniwa'' have been found only at 16 tumuli, but the majority of the tumuli remain unexcavated.
Artifacts recovered from the site are exhibited at the {{nihongo|'''Chiba Prefectural Bōsō Mura no Fudoki no Oka Museum'''|千葉県立房総のむら風土記の丘資料館|Chiba-kenritsu Bōsō Mura no Fudoki no Oka Shiryōkan}}. The site is a five minute walk from the Fudoki no Oka Kitaguchi bus stop on the Chiba Kotsu Bus from [[Ajiki Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Narita Line]]<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Iwaya Kofun== The Iwaya Kofun is Kofun No. 105 in the cluster. It is a square-sided tumulus {{nihongo|''{{ill|Square Kofun|lt=hōfun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}''|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}) and is the largest tumulus in the cluster. The tumulus is approximately 78 meters on each side, and is built in three tiers similar to a step pyramid, with a total height of 13 meters. Inside are two lateral-type stone-lined [[burial chamber]]s constructed of blocks of soft limestone containing many fossilized shells. The tumulus has a double moat, enclosing an area measuring 108 meters east-to-west by 96 meters north-to-south. Since the entrance has been open since the [[Edo period]], no grave goods have been discovered.

Artifacts recovered from the site are exhibited at the {{nihongo|'''Chiba Prefectural Bōsō Mura no Fudoki no Oka Museum'''|千葉県立房総のむら風土記の丘資料館|Chiba-kenritsu Bōsō Mura no Fudoki no Oka Shiryōkan}}. The site is a five minute walk from the Fudoki no Oka Kitaguchi bus stop on the Chiba Kotsu Bus from [[Ajiki Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Narita Line]]<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Iwaya Kofun== The Iwaya Kofun is Kofun No. 105 in the cluster. It is a square-sided tumulus {{nihongo|''{{ill|Square Kofun|lt=hōfun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}''|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}) and is the largest tumulus in the cluster. The tumulus is approximately 78 meters on each side, and is built in three tiers similar to a step pyramid, with a total height of 13 meters. Inside are two lateral-type stone-lined [[burial chamber]]s constructed of blocks of soft limestone containing many fossilized shells. The tumulus has a double moat, enclosing an area measuring 108 meters east-to-west by 96 meters north-to-south. Since the entrance has been open since the [[Edo period]], no grave goods have been discovered.

{{Infobox ancient site | name = Ryūgakuji kofun cluster | native_name =龍角寺古墳群 | native_name_lang = | image = Iwaya-kofun-zenkei.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Iwaya kofun in the Ryūgakuji kofun cluster | map_type = Japan Chiba Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ryūgakuji kofun cluster | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|35|49|16|N|140|16|38|E|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Sakae, Chiba|Sakae]] / [[Narita, Chiba|Narita]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kantō region]] | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (park and museum) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
{{nihongo|'''Ryūgakuji kofun cluster'''|龍角寺古墳群|Ryūgakuji kofun-gun}} is a group of [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|burial mound]]s located in what is now the Ryukakuji neighborhood of the town of [[Sakae, Chiba|Sakae]] and extending into the city of [[Narita, Chiba|Narita]], [[Chiba Prefecture]] in the [[Kantō region]] of [[Japan]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1941, with the area extended in 2009.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/211990|title=龍角寺古墳群・岩屋古墳 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref> Containing 114 tumuli, it is the largest cluster of ''kofun'' in the Kantō region. The National Historic Site designation also singles out Kofun No. 105, which is named the {{nihongo|'''Iwaya kofun'''|岩屋古墳|}}.
==Overview== The Ryūgakuji kofun cluster dates from the late Kofun period (end of the 6th century to the end of the 7th century AD), and is located on what was once the northeastern coast of Lake Inbanuma. The area is part of the Shimōsa Plateau with an elevation of about 30 meters above sea level. The site is relatively narrow, and the tumuli are distributed in strips over the narrow plateau from the northwest to the southeast for about 1.5 kilometers. The total number of tumuli currently confirmed is 114, although many may have been destroyed by road construction and urban encroachment, and others may yet to be discovered.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> The types of these 114 tumuli are distributed as follows:
{| class="wikitable" |+ Ryūgakuji Kofun Cluster |- ! type !! Narita !! Sakae !! Total |- | {{Ill|Round Kofun|lt=Domed|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun}}) || 19 || 52 || 71 |- | {{ill|Square Kofun|lt=Square|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} ({{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}) || 1 || 5 || 6 |- | [[Zenpokoenfun|Keyhole]] ({{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}) || 9 || 28 || 37 |- | TOTAL || 29 || 85 || 114 |- |}
Most of the tumuli are well preserved and more than 70 percent are located on prefectural lands. At present, the oldest is believed to be scallop-shaped Kofun No.101, which was constructed in the second quarter of the 6th century AD based on the type of ''[[haniwa]]'' discovered. The later mounds are concentrated in the northeast corner of the site, and include the Sengenyama Kofun, which is believed to be the last ''kofun'' built in Chiba Prefecture. From these dates, it is clear that the tumulus-building culture continued well after the introduction of [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]] to the area in the [[Hakuho period]] as evidenced by the nearby ruins of the [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple]] of [[Ryūkaku-ji]]. Many of the tumuli are small, with a length of 20 to 30 meters in the case of keyhole-shaped tumuli, and a diameter of 10 to 20 meters in the case of many dome-shaped burial mounds.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
Some of the larger mounds were found to have more than one burial chamber, or had been reused for a subsequent burial some period after its original construction. ''Haniwa'' have been found only at 16 tumuli, but the majority of the tumuli remain unexcavated.
Artifacts recovered from the site are exhibited at the {{nihongo|'''Chiba Prefectural Bōsō Mura no Fudoki no Oka Museum'''|千葉県立房総のむら風土記の丘資料館|Chiba-kenritsu Bōsō Mura no Fudoki no Oka Shiryōkan}}. The site is a five minute walk from the Fudoki no Oka Kitaguchi bus stop on the Chiba Kotsu Bus from [[Ajiki Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Narita Line]]<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Iwaya Kofun== The Iwaya Kofun is Kofun No. 105 in the cluster. It is a square-sided tumulus {{nihongo|''{{ill|Square Kofun|lt=hōfun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}''|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}) and is the largest tumulus in the cluster. The tumulus is approximately 78 meters on each side, and is built in three tiers similar to a step pyramid, with a total height of 13 meters. Inside are two lateral-type stone-lined [[burial chamber]]s constructed of blocks of soft limestone containing many fossilized shells. The tumulus has a double moat, enclosing an area measuring 108 meters east-to-west by 96 meters north-to-south. Since the entrance has been open since the [[Edo period]], no grave goods have been discovered.

It is a square-sided tumulus {{nihongo|''{{ill|Square Kofun|lt=hōfun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}''|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}) and is the largest tumulus in the cluster. The tumulus is approximately 78 meters on each side, and is built in three tiers similar to a step pyramid, with a total height of 13 meters. Inside are two lateral-type stone-lined [[burial chamber]]s constructed of blocks of soft limestone containing many fossilized shells. The tumulus has a double moat, enclosing an area measuring 108 meters east-to-west by 96 meters north-to-south. Since the entrance has been open since the [[Edo period]], no grave goods have been discovered.

{{Infobox ancient site | name = Ryūgakuji kofun cluster | native_name =龍角寺古墳群 | native_name_lang = | image = Iwaya-kofun-zenkei.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Iwaya kofun in the Ryūgakuji kofun cluster | map_type = Japan Chiba Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ryūgakuji kofun cluster | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|35|49|16|N|140|16|38|E|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Sakae, Chiba|Sakae]] / [[Narita, Chiba|Narita]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kantō region]] | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (park and museum) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
{{nihongo|'''Ryūgakuji kofun cluster'''|龍角寺古墳群|Ryūgakuji kofun-gun}} is a group of [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|burial mound]]s located in what is now the Ryukakuji neighborhood of the town of [[Sakae, Chiba|Sakae]] and extending into the city of [[Narita, Chiba|Narita]], [[Chiba Prefecture]] in the [[Kantō region]] of [[Japan]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1941, with the area extended in 2009.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/211990|title=龍角寺古墳群・岩屋古墳 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref> Containing 114 tumuli, it is the largest cluster of ''kofun'' in the Kantō region. The National Historic Site designation also singles out Kofun No. 105, which is named the {{nihongo|'''Iwaya kofun'''|岩屋古墳|}}.
==Overview== The Ryūgakuji kofun cluster dates from the late Kofun period (end of the 6th century to the end of the 7th century AD), and is located on what was once the northeastern coast of Lake Inbanuma. The area is part of the Shimōsa Plateau with an elevation of about 30 meters above sea level. The site is relatively narrow, and the tumuli are distributed in strips over the narrow plateau from the northwest to the southeast for about 1.5 kilometers. The total number of tumuli currently confirmed is 114, although many may have been destroyed by road construction and urban encroachment, and others may yet to be discovered.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> The types of these 114 tumuli are distributed as follows:
{| class="wikitable" |+ Ryūgakuji Kofun Cluster |- ! type !! Narita !! Sakae !! Total |- | {{Ill|Round Kofun|lt=Domed|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun}}) || 19 || 52 || 71 |- | {{ill|Square Kofun|lt=Square|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} ({{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}) || 1 || 5 || 6 |- | [[Zenpokoenfun|Keyhole]] ({{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}) || 9 || 28 || 37 |- | TOTAL || 29 || 85 || 114 |- |}
Most of the tumuli are well preserved and more than 70 percent are located on prefectural lands. At present, the oldest is believed to be scallop-shaped Kofun No.101, which was constructed in the second quarter of the 6th century AD based on the type of ''[[haniwa]]'' discovered. The later mounds are concentrated in the northeast corner of the site, and include the Sengenyama Kofun, which is believed to be the last ''kofun'' built in Chiba Prefecture. From these dates, it is clear that the tumulus-building culture continued well after the introduction of [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]] to the area in the [[Hakuho period]] as evidenced by the nearby ruins of the [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple]] of [[Ryūkaku-ji]]. Many of the tumuli are small, with a length of 20 to 30 meters in the case of keyhole-shaped tumuli, and a diameter of 10 to 20 meters in the case of many dome-shaped burial mounds.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
Some of the larger mounds were found to have more than one burial chamber, or had been reused for a subsequent burial some period after its original construction. ''Haniwa'' have been found only at 16 tumuli, but the majority of the tumuli remain unexcavated.
Artifacts recovered from the site are exhibited at the {{nihongo|'''Chiba Prefectural Bōsō Mura no Fudoki no Oka Museum'''|千葉県立房総のむら風土記の丘資料館|Chiba-kenritsu Bōsō Mura no Fudoki no Oka Shiryōkan}}. The site is a five minute walk from the Fudoki no Oka Kitaguchi bus stop on the Chiba Kotsu Bus from [[Ajiki Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Narita Line]]<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Iwaya Kofun== The Iwaya Kofun is Kofun No. 105 in the cluster. It is a square-sided tumulus {{nihongo|''{{ill|Square Kofun|lt=hōfun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}''|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}) and is the largest tumulus in the cluster. The tumulus is approximately 78 meters on each side, and is built in three tiers similar to a step pyramid, with a total height of 13 meters. Inside are two lateral-type stone-lined [[burial chamber]]s constructed of blocks of soft limestone containing many fossilized shells. The tumulus has a double moat, enclosing an area measuring 108 meters east-to-west by 96 meters north-to-south. Since the entrance has been open since the [[Edo period]], no grave goods have been discovered.
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Chiba)]]

{{Infobox ancient site | name = Ryūgakuji kofun cluster | native_name =龍角寺古墳群 | native_name_lang = | image = Iwaya-kofun-zenkei.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Iwaya kofun in the Ryūgakuji kofun cluster | map_type = Japan Chiba Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ryūgakuji kofun cluster | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|35|49|16|N|140|16|38|E|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Sakae, Chiba|Sakae]] / [[Narita, Chiba|Narita]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kantō region]] | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (park and museum) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
{{nihongo|'''Ryūgakuji kofun cluster'''|龍角寺古墳群|Ryūgakuji kofun-gun}} is a group of [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofun]]s located in what is now the Ryukakuji neighborhood of the town of [[Sakae, Chiba|Sakae]] and extending into the city of [[Narita, Chiba|Narita]], [[Chiba Prefecture]] in the [[Kantō region]] of [[Japan]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1941, with the area extended in 2009.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/211990|title=龍角寺古墳群・岩屋古墳 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref> Containing 114 tumuli, it is the largest cluster of ''kofun'' in the Kantō region. The National Historic Site designation also singles out Kofun No. 105, which is named the {{nihongo|'''Iwaya kofun'''|岩屋古墳|}}.
==Overview== The Ryūgakuji kofun cluster dates from the late Kofun period (end of the 6th century to the end of the 7th century AD), and is located on what was once the northeastern coast of Lake Inbanuma. The area is part of the Shimōsa Plateau with an elevation of about 30 meters above sea level. The site is relatively narrow, and the tumuli are distributed in strips over the narrow plateau from the northwest to the southeast for about 1.5 kilometers. The total number of tumuli currently confirmed is 114, although many may have been destroyed by road construction and urban encroachment, and others may yet to be discovered.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> The types of these 114 tumuli are distributed as follows:
{| class="wikitable" |+ Ryūgakuji Kofun Cluster |- ! type !! Narita !! Sakae !! Total |- | {{Ill|Round Kofun|lt=Domed|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun}}) || 19 || 52 || 71 |- | {{ill|Square Kofun|lt=Square|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} ({{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}) || 1 || 5 || 6 |- | [[Zenpokoenfun|Keyhole]] ({{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}) || 9 || 28 || 37 |- | TOTAL || 29 || 85 || 114 |- |}
Most of the tumuli are well preserved and more than 70 percent are located on prefectural lands. At present, the oldest is believed to be scallop-shaped Kofun No.101, which was constructed in the second quarter of the 6th century AD based on the type of ''[[haniwa]]'' discovered. The later mounds are concentrated in the northeast corner of the site, and include the Sengenyama Kofun, which is believed to be the last ''kofun'' built in Chiba Prefecture. From these dates, it is clear that the tumulus-building culture continued well after the introduction of [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]] to the area in the [[Hakuho period]] as evidenced by the nearby ruins of the [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple]] of [[Ryūkaku-ji]]. Many of the tumuli are small, with a length of 20 to 30 meters in the case of keyhole-shaped tumuli, and a diameter of 10 to 20 meters in the case of many dome-shaped Kofuns.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
Some of the larger mounds were found to have more than one burial chamber, or had been reused for a subsequent burial some period after its original construction. ''Haniwa'' have been found only at 16 tumuli, but the majority of the tumuli remain unexcavated.
Artifacts recovered from the site are exhibited at the {{nihongo|'''Chiba Prefectural Bōsō Mura no Fudoki no Oka Museum'''|千葉県立房総のむら風土記の丘資料館|Chiba-kenritsu Bōsō Mura no Fudoki no Oka Shiryōkan}}. The site is a five minute walk from the Fudoki no Oka Kitaguchi bus stop on the Chiba Kotsu Bus from [[Ajiki Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Narita Line]]<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Iwaya Kofun== The Iwaya Kofun is Kofun No. 105 in the cluster. It is a square-sided tumulus {{nihongo|''{{ill|Square Kofun|lt=hōfun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}''|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}) and is the largest tumulus in the cluster. The tumulus is approximately 78 meters on each side, and is built in three tiers similar to a step pyramid, with a total height of 13 meters. Inside are two lateral-type stone-lined [[burial chamber]]s constructed of blocks of soft limestone containing many fossilized shells. The tumulus has a double moat, enclosing an area measuring 108 meters east-to-west by 96 meters north-to-south. Since the entrance has been open since the [[Edo period]], no grave goods have been discovered.
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Chiba)]]

{{Infobox ancient site | name = Ryūgakuji kofun cluster | native_name =龍角寺古墳群 | native_name_lang = | image = Iwaya-kofun-zenkei.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Iwaya kofun in the Ryūgakuji kofun cluster | map_type = Japan Chiba Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ryūgakuji kofun cluster | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|35|49|16|N|140|16|38|E|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Sakae, Chiba|Sakae]] / [[Narita, Chiba|Narita]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kantō region]] | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (park and museum) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
{{nihongo|'''Ryūgakuji kofun cluster'''|龍角寺古墳群|Ryūgakuji kofun-gun}} is a group of [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofun]]s located in what is now the Ryukakuji neighborhood of the town of [[Sakae, Chiba|Sakae]] and extending into the city of [[Narita, Chiba|Narita]], [[Chiba Prefecture]] in the [[Kantō region]] of [[Japan]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1941, with the area extended in 2009.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/211990|title=龍角寺古墳群・岩屋古墳 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref> Containing 114 tumuli, it is the largest cluster of ''kofun'' in the Kantō region. The National Historic Site designation also singles out Kofun No. 105, which is named the {{nihongo|'''Iwaya kofun'''|岩屋古墳|}}.
==Overview== The Ryūgakuji kofun cluster dates from the late Kofun period (end of the 6th century to the end of the 7th century AD), and is located on what was once the northeastern coast of Lake Inbanuma. The area is part of the Shimōsa Plateau with an elevation of about 30 meters above sea level. The site is relatively narrow, and the tumuli are distributed in strips over the narrow plateau from the northwest to the southeast for about 1.5 kilometers. The total number of tumuli currently confirmed is 114, although many may have been destroyed by road construction and urban encroachment, and others may yet to be discovered.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> The types of these 114 tumuli are distributed as follows:
{| class="wikitable" |+ Ryūgakuji Kofun Cluster |- ! type !! Narita !! Sakae !! Total |- | {{Ill|Round Kofun|lt=Domed|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun}}) || 19 || 52 || 71 |- | {{ill|Square Kofun|lt=Square|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} ({{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}) || 1 || 5 || 6 |- | [[Zenpokoenfun|Keyhole]] ({{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}) || 9 || 28 || 37 |- | TOTAL || 29 || 85 || 114 |- |}
Most of the tumuli are well preserved and more than 70 percent are located on prefectural lands. At present, the oldest is believed to be scallop-shaped Kofun No.101, which was constructed in the second quarter of the 6th century AD based on the type of ''[[haniwa]]'' discovered. The later mounds are concentrated in the northeast corner of the site, and include the Sengenyama Kofun, which is believed to be the last ''kofun'' built in Chiba Prefecture. From these dates, it is clear that the tumulus-building culture continued well after the introduction of [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]] to the area in the [[Hakuho period]] as evidenced by the nearby ruins of the [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple]] of [[Ryūkaku-ji]]. Many of the tumuli are small, with a length of 20 to 30 meters in the case of keyhole-shaped tumuli, and a diameter of 10 to 20 meters in the case of many dome-shaped Kofuns.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
Some of the larger mounds were found to have more than one burial chamber, or had been reused for a subsequent burial some period after its original construction. ''Haniwa'' have been found only at 16 tumuli, but the majority of the tumuli remain unexcavated.
Artifacts recovered from the site are exhibited at the {{nihongo|'''Chiba Prefectural Bōsō Mura no Fudoki no Oka Museum'''|千葉県立房総のむら風土記の丘資料館|Chiba-kenritsu Bōsō Mura no Fudoki no Oka Shiryōkan}}. The site is a five minute walk from the Fudoki no Oka Kitaguchi bus stop on the Chiba Kotsu Bus from [[Ajiki Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Narita Line]]<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Iwaya Kofun== The Iwaya Kofun is Kofun No. 105 in the cluster. It is a square-sided tumulus {{nihongo|''{{ill|Square Kofun|lt=hōfun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}''|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}) and is the largest tumulus in the cluster. The tumulus is approximately 78 meters on each side, and is built in three tiers similar to a step pyramid, with a total height of 13 meters. Inside are two lateral-type stone-lined [[burial chamber]]s constructed of blocks of soft limestone containing many fossilized shells. The tumulus has a double moat, enclosing an area measuring 108 meters east-to-west by 96 meters north-to-south. Since the entrance has been open since the [[Edo period]], no grave goods have been discovered.
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Chiba)]]

revision.text:
{{Infobox ancient site | name = Ryūgakuji kofun cluster | native_name =龍角寺古墳群 | native_name_lang = | image = Iwaya-kofun-zenkei.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Iwaya kofun in the Ryūgakuji kofun cluster | map_type = Japan Chiba Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ryūgakuji kofun cluster | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|35|49|16|N|140|16|38|E|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Sakae, Chiba|Sakae]] / [[Narita, Chiba|Narita]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kantō region]] | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (park and museum) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
{{nihongo|'''Ryūgakuji kofun cluster'''|龍角寺古墳群|Ryūgakuji kofun-gun}} is a group of [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofun]]s located in what is now the Ryukakuji neighborhood of the town of [[Sakae, Chiba|Sakae]] and extending into the city of [[Narita, Chiba|Narita]], [[Chiba Prefecture]] in the [[Kantō region]] of [[Japan]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1941, with the area extended in 2009.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/211990|title=龍角寺古墳群・岩屋古墳 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref> Containing 114 tumuli, it is the largest cluster of ''kofun'' in the Kantō region. The National Historic Site designation also singles out Kofun No. 105, which is named the {{nihongo|'''Iwaya kofun'''|岩屋古墳|}}.
==Overview== The Ryūgakuji kofun cluster dates from the late Kofun period (end of the 6th century to the end of the 7th century AD), and is located on what was once the northeastern coast of Lake Inbanuma. The area is part of the Shimōsa Plateau with an elevation of about 30 meters above sea level. The site is relatively narrow, and the tumuli are distributed in strips over the narrow plateau from the northwest to the southeast for about 1.5 kilometers. The total number of tumuli currently confirmed is 114, although many may have been destroyed by road construction and urban encroachment, and others may yet to be discovered.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> The types of these 114 tumuli are distributed as follows:
{| class="wikitable" |+ Ryūgakuji Kofun Cluster |- ! type !! Narita !! Sakae !! Total |- | {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Round Kofun|lt=Domed|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}) || 19 || 52 || 71 |- | {{ill|Square Kofun|lt=Square|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} ({{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}) || 1 || 5 || 6 |- | [[Zenpokoenfun|Keyhole]] ({{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}) || 9 || 28 || 37 |- | TOTAL || 29 || 85 || 114 |- |}
Most of the tumuli are well preserved and more than 70 percent are located on prefectural lands. At present, the oldest is believed to be scallop-shaped Kofun No.101, which was constructed in the second quarter of the 6th century AD based on the type of ''[[haniwa]]'' discovered. The later mounds are concentrated in the northeast corner of the site, and include the Sengenyama Kofun, which is believed to be the last ''kofun'' built in Chiba Prefecture. From these dates, it is clear that the tumulus-building culture continued well after the introduction of [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]] to the area in the [[Hakuho period]] as evidenced by the nearby ruins of the [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple]] of [[Ryūkaku-ji]]. Many of the tumuli are small, with a length of 20 to 30 meters in the case of keyhole-shaped tumuli, and a diameter of 10 to 20 meters in the case of many dome-shaped Kofuns.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
Some of the larger mounds were found to have more than one burial chamber, or had been reused for a subsequent burial some period after its original construction. ''Haniwa'' have been found only at 16 tumuli, but the majority of the tumuli remain unexcavated.
Artifacts recovered from the site are exhibited at the {{nihongo|'''Chiba Prefectural Bōsō Mura no Fudoki no Oka Museum'''|千葉県立房総のむら風土記の丘資料館|Chiba-kenritsu Bōsō Mura no Fudoki no Oka Shiryōkan}}. The site is a five minute walk from the Fudoki no Oka Kitaguchi bus stop on the Chiba Kotsu Bus from [[Ajiki Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Narita Line]]<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Iwaya Kofun== The Iwaya Kofun is Kofun No. 105 in the cluster. It is a square-sided tumulus {{nihongo|''{{ill|Square Kofun|lt=hōfun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}''|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}) and is the largest tumulus in the cluster. The tumulus is approximately 78 meters on each side, and is built in three tiers similar to a step pyramid, with a total height of 13 meters. Inside are two lateral-type stone-lined [[burial chamber]]s constructed of blocks of soft limestone containing many fossilized shells. The tumulus has a double moat, enclosing an area measuring 108 meters east-to-west by 96 meters north-to-south. Since the entrance has been open since the [[Edo period]], no grave goods have been discovered.
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Chiba)]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{commons Category:kk]][[Category:-inline|Ryukakuji Kofun Group}} *[https://www.pref.chiba.lg.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunkazai/n411-017.html Chiba Prefecture official site] {{in lang|ja}} *[http://www.town.sakae.chiba.jp/index.php?code=874 Sakae Town official home page] {{in lang|ja}} *[http://www2.chiba-muse.or.jp/www/MURA/contents/1519312671176/index.html Chiba Prefectural Bōsō Mura no Fudoki no Oka Museum] {{in lang|ja}}

{{Infobox ancient site | name = Ryūgakuji kofun cluster | native_name =龍角寺古墳群 | native_name_lang = | image = Iwaya-kofun-zenkei.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Iwaya kofun in the Ryūgakuji kofun cluster | map_type = Japan Chiba Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ryūgakuji kofun cluster | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|35|49|16|N|140|16|38|E|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Sakae, Chiba|Sakae]] / [[Narita, Chiba|Narita]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kantō region]] | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (park and museum) | Historic Site = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
{{nihongo|'''Ryūgakuji kofun cluster'''|龍角寺古墳群|Ryūgakuji kofun-gun}} is a group of [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofun]]s located in what is now the Ryukakuji neighborhood of the town of [[Sakae, Chiba|Sakae]] and extending into the city of [[Narita, Chiba|Narita]], [[Chiba Prefecture]] in the [[Kantō region]] of [[Japan]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1941, with the area extended in 2009.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/211990|title=龍角寺古墳群・岩屋古墳 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref> Containing 114 tumuli, it is the largest cluster of ''kofun'' in the Kantō region. The National Historic Site designation also singles out Kofun No. 105, which is named the {{nihongo|'''Iwaya kofun'''|岩屋古墳|}}.
==Overview== The Ryūgakuji kofun cluster dates from the late Kofun period (end of the 6th century to the end of the 7th century AD), and is located on what was once the northeastern coast of Lake Inbanuma. The area is part of the Shimōsa Plateau with an elevation of about 30 meters above sea level. The site is relatively narrow, and the tumuli are distributed in strips over the narrow plateau from the northwest to the southeast for about 1.5 kilometers. The total number of tumuli currently confirmed is 114, although many may have been destroyed by road construction and urban encroachment, and others may yet to be discovered.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> The types of these 114 tumuli are distributed as follows:
{| class="wikitable" |+ Ryūgakuji Kofun Cluster |- ! type !! Narita !! Sakae !! Total |- | {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Round Kofun|lt=Domed|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}) || 19 || 52 || 71 |- | {{ill|Square Kofun|lt=Square|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun}} ({{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun}}) || 1 || 5 || 6 |- | [[Zenpokoenfun|Keyhole]] ({{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}) || 9 || 28 || 37 |- | TOTAL || 29 || 85 || 114 |- |}
Most of the tumuli are well preserved and more than 70 percent are located on prefectural lands. At present, the oldest is believed to be scallop-shaped Kofun No.101, which was constructed in the second quarter of the 6th century AD based on the type of ''[[haniwa]]'' discovered. The later mounds are concentrated in the northeast corner of the site, and include the Sengenyama Kofun, which is believed to be the last ''kofun'' built in Chiba Prefecture. From these dates, it is clear that the tumulus-building culture continued well after the introduction of [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]] to the area in the [[Hakuho period]] as evidenced by the nearby ruins of the [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple]] of [[Ryūkaku-ji]]. Many of the tumuli are small, with a length of 20 to 30 meters in the case of keyhole-shaped tumuli, and a diameter of 10 to 20 meters in the case of many dome-shaped Kofuns.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
Some of the larger mounds were found to have more than one burial chamber, or had been reused for a subsequent burial some period after its original construction. ''Haniwa'' have been found only at 16 tumuli, but the majority of the tumuli remain unexcavated.
Artifacts recovered from the site are exhibited at the {{nihongo|'''Chiba Prefectural Bōsō Mura no Fudoki no Oka Museum'''|千葉県立房総のむら風土記の丘資料館|Chiba-kenritsu Bōsō Mura no Fudoki no Oka Shiryōkan}}. The site is a five minute walk from the Fudoki no Oka Kitaguchi bus stop on the Chiba Kotsu Bus from [[Ajiki Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Narita Line]]<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Iwaya Kofun== The Iwaya Kofun is Kofun No. 105 in the cluster. It is a square-sided tumulus {{nihongo|''{{ill|Square Kofun|lt=hōfun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun}}''|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun}}) and is the largest tumulus in the cluster. The tumulus is approximately 78 meters on each side, and is built in three tiers similar to a step pyramid, with a total height of 13 meters. Inside are two lateral-type stone-lined [[burial chamber]]s constructed of blocks of soft limestone containing many fossilized shells. The tumulus has a double moat, enclosing an area measuring 108 meters east-to-west by 96 meters north-to-south. Since the entrance has been open since the [[Edo period]], no grave goods have been discovered.
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Chiba)]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{commons Category:kk]][[Category:-inline|Ryukakuji Kofun Group}} *[https://www.pref.chiba.lg.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunkazai/n411-017.html Chiba Prefecture official site] {{in lang|ja}} *[http://www.town.sakae.chiba.jp/index.php?code=874 Sakae Town official home page] {{in lang|ja}} *[http://www2.chiba-muse.or.jp/www/MURA/contents/1519312671176/index.html Chiba Prefectural Bōsō Mura no Fudoki no Oka Museum] {{in lang|ja}}

The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1941, with the area extended in 2009.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/211990|title=龍角寺古墳群・岩屋古墳 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref> Containing 114 tumuli, it is the largest cluster of ''kofun'' in the Kantō region. {| class="wikitable" |+ Ryūgakuji Kofun Cluster |- ! type !! Narita !! Sakae !! Total |- | {{ill|Round Kofun|lt=Domed|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}) || 19 || 52 || 71 |- | {{ill|Square Kofun|lt=Square|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} ({{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun}}) || 1 || 5 || 6 |- | [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Keyhole]] ({{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}) || 9 || 28 || 37 |- | TOTAL || 29 || 85 || 114 |- |}
Most of the tumuli are well preserved and more than 70 percent are located on prefectural lands. At present, the oldest is believed to be scallop-shaped Kofun No.101, which was constructed in the second quarter of the 6th century AD based on the type of ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]'' discovered. The later mounds are concentrated in the northeast corner of the site, and include the Sengenyama Kofun, which is believed to be the last ''kofun'' built in Chiba Prefecture. From these dates, it is clear that the tumulus-building culture continued well after the introduction of [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]] to the area in the [[:en:Hakuho period|Hakuho period]] as evidenced by the nearby ruins of the [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple]] of [[:en:Ryūkaku-ji|Ryūkaku-ji]]. The site is a five minute walk from the Fudoki no Oka Kitaguchi bus stop on the Chiba Kotsu Bus from [[:en:Ajiki Station|Ajiki Station]] on the [[:en:East Japan Railway Company|East Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Narita Line|Narita Line]]<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Iwaya Kofun== The Iwaya Kofun is Kofun No. 105 in the cluster. It is a square-sided tumulus {{nihongo|''{{ill|Square Kofun|lt=hōfun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun}}) and is the largest tumulus in the cluster. Since the entrance has been open since the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], no grave goods have been discovered.
